DEMURRER to COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Apr-29-2025 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

Appearances (via Zoom videoconference):
Atty. EMILE DAVIS, Esq. of Dolan Law Firm, PC (tel: 415-421-2800) for Plaintiff.
Atty. NICHOLAS DYESS, Esq. of the California Department of Transportation (tel: 510-433-9100) for Defendant Caltrans.
Atty. LAUREN CANTER, Esq. of the Office of The Attorney General of California (tel: 415-510-3493) for Defendant California Highway Patrol.

Hearing Re: DEFENDANT CALIFORNIA HIGHWAY PATROL's DEMURRER to COMPLAINT.

Matter argued and submitted. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Defendant California Highway Patrol (CHP) demurs to count one of Theresa Bayliss-Pedrotti's complaint alleging a cause of action against CHP for dangerous condition of public property on the Bay Bridge. The case arises out of an accident. According to the complaint, an intoxicated person ran out of gas on the bridge and stopped in a traffic lane, and Bayliss-Pedrotti's daughter's car ran into the intoxicated person's stopped car. While the daughter was stopped to exchange information with the intoxicated person, a third car struck her, and the accident was fatal. CHP (and Caltrans, which has answered the complaint) are alleged to have maintained a dangerous condition of public property because they own or control the stretch of I-80 where the accidents occurred, and because they undertook to monitor the stretch and keep it clear of accidents.

CHP contends first that it does not own or control the stretch of I-80 where the accidents occurred, relying on a case that distinguished CHP's and Caltrans's statutory roles. (See Greyhound Lines v Department of California Highway Patrol (2013) 213 Cal. App. 4th, 1129.) That is ordinarily true, but Bayliss-Pedrotti offers to plead facts that CHP partners with Caltrans to jointly manage and control traffic operations on the Bay Bridge and to oversee management of collisions like this one. The court grants Bayliss-Pedrotti leave to amend.

CHP contends that leave would be futile to permit Bayliss-Pedrotti leave to amend because even if CHP owned or controlled the stretch of I-80, she fails to state a claim for dangerous condition of public property in the fact of a blocked lane resulting from an accident. CHP relies on Zelig v. County of Los Angeles (2002) 27 Cal.4th 1112 [failure to prevent criminal acts of third parties is not a dangerous condition of public property]. But Zelig emphasized that "liability is imposed only when there is some defect in the property itself and a causal connection is established between the defect and the injury." (Id. at p. 1135.) Here, Bayliss-Pedrotti alleges a causal connection, e.g. in the lack of "warning signage, turn outs, [or] signage advising what to do in the event of a collision." (Compl. para. 30(a).) CHP's remaining arguments are not susceptible to resolution on demurrer.

The court denies Bayliss-Pedrotti's request for judicial notice of the truth of facts stated in a government website but grants her leave to amend to allege facts concerning CHP's undertaking to control the Bay Bridge.

Counsel for the prevailing party is to prepare and submit a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the Court's ruling, with a cover letter reflecting compliance with CRC 3.1312.

Judge: Christine Van Aken; Clerk: V. Da Fonseca; Not reported. (301/CVA)

DEFENDANT CALIFORNIA HIGHWAY PATROL'S DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT (PART 2 OF 2 OF THE TENTATIVE RULING)
Off Calendar Apr-29-2025 Off Calendar

DEMURRER to COMPLAINT
